heroOverview: Periodontosis — now classified as localised or generalised aggressive periodontitis — is a rapidly destructive form of periodontal disease that typically begins around puberty, attacking the bone and attachment fibres supporting the permanent teeth far faster than the chronic plaque-associated form. While chronic periodontitis may take decades to cause tooth loss, aggressive periodontitis can produce severe bone destruction within 2–3 years in a patient with comparatively little plaque or inflammation, reflecting an underlying defect in innate immune defence rather than simply poor oral hygiene. The condition affects roughly 0.1–0.2% of adolescents in Western populations and up to 2–5% in some African and Middle Eastern populations, with a strong familial clustering consistent with a primarily genetic susceptibility. Neutrophil function defects — impairing the host's ability to kill Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans (Aa), the key pathogen — appear to be central to pathogenesis. Without early recognition and treatment, affected individuals face tooth loss before age 25, making timely diagnosis by dentists examining adolescents critically important.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 0.1–0.2% of adolescents in Western populations; prevalence reaches 2–5% in parts of Africa and the Middle East with strong familial aggregation
keyStats.demographics: Onset typically at puberty (11–17 years); localised form more common in females; generalised form affects both sexes near equally; strong family history in approximately 40–50% of affected individuals
keyStats.avgAge: First diagnosed between ages 11 and 17, often presenting with bone loss around first permanent molars and incisors by early adolescence
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated millions affected worldwide; a major cause of early tooth loss in adolescents globally, particularly in sub-Saharan African and Middle Eastern populations

definition: Periodontosis (ICD-10: K05.4), now more accurately termed aggressive periodontitis in contemporary classification systems (2017 World Workshop), is a group of uncommon but severe forms of periodontal disease characterised by rapid, destructive loss of alveolar bone and periodontal attachment occurring predominantly in otherwise healthy adolescents and young adults, with bone loss out of proportion to the local microbial deposits (plaque and calculus). The 2017 classification merged aggressive and chronic periodontitis under a unified staging and grading system, with grade C (rapidly progressing) equivalent to the historical aggressive periodontitis diagnosis. Periodontosis was the earlier twentieth-century term for the same clinical entity, retained in ICD-10. The disease has two main clinical presentations: localised aggressive periodontitis (LAP), in which destruction is concentrated at the first permanent molars and incisors (the classic first-molar/incisor distribution), and generalised aggressive periodontitis (GAP), where bone destruction affects at least three teeth other than first molars and incisors and progresses rapidly across the entire dentition. The key pathogen is Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans (Aa) leukotoxin-producing strains, which specifically impair host neutrophil function; Porphyromonas gingivalis and Tannerella forsythia are important co-pathogens in generalised cases. Genetic studies consistently show autosomal dominant patterns with incomplete penetrance in many families, and several gene variants affecting neutrophil chemotaxis, receptor function, and cytokine signalling have been identified. The condition is broadly distinguished from chronic (plaque-induced) periodontitis by: earlier age of onset; family aggregation; disproportionate bone loss relative to biofilm; specific microbial profile; and demonstrable defects in neutrophil and monocyte function in affected patients.

typesClassification.description: Classic juvenile periodontitis restricted to the first permanent molars and permanent incisors; the disease begins in the molar or incisor region at puberty, producing deep pockets and angular bone loss around these specific teeth while other teeth show minimal involvement. The bone loss pattern is mirror-image and highly characteristic on radiographs. Aa is the dominant pathogen.

Affects at least three teeth beyond the classic first molar/incisor pattern, often involving the entire dentition; typically affects young adults aged 17–35 and progresses episodically in active bursts. Clinical gingival inflammation is more pronounced than in the localised form, and multiple periodontal pathogens including P. gingivalis are more prominent.

typesClassification.type: Periodontosis as a feature of systemic disease
typesClassification.description: Aggressive periodontal destruction clinically indistinguishable from LAP/GAP occurs as the dominant feature of Papillon-Lefèvre syndrome (cathepsin C deficiency), Chédiak-Higashi syndrome, severe congenital neutropenia, and leukocyte adhesion deficiency. In these conditions, loss of the primary dentition in early childhood precedes the permanent dentition destruction.

primarySymptoms: Deep periodontal pockets — 6 mm or greater — around the first permanent molars and incisors, often discovered on probing during a routine dental check-up in an adolescent.

Angular (vertical) bone loss on radiographs, characteristically in a mirror-image pattern around the mesial surfaces of first molars and central incisors, disproportionate to the amount of visible plaque.
Tooth mobility developing in adolescence — unexpected looseness of a first molar or incisor in a young person, which should always prompt periodontal assessment.
Gingival recession at affected teeth, often without the bright-red, swollen gingivae typical of plaque-induced gingivitis, reflecting a non-inflammatory immune-mediated destructive process.
Dull aching or pressure around the affected teeth during biting, as the reduced bone support transfers load stress to the remaining periodontium.
Migration (drifting) of maxillary incisors — flaring or spacing of the front upper teeth as alveolar bone support is lost and the teeth tip forward under lip pressure.
Orthodontic-appearing gaps between incisors that develop without orthodontic treatment, representing pathological drifting from bone loss rather than a spacing abnormality.
Halitosis (bad breath) from the periodontal pockets, in which Aa and other anaerobic bacteria produce volatile sulphur compounds.
Tenderness to percussion of affected teeth, indicating acute periodontal ligament involvement or early abscess formation within deep pockets.
In advanced cases, deep pain and visible gingival swelling around the first molar in an adolescent with an acute lateral periodontal abscess from a pocket that has become obstructed.
earlyWarningSigns: A parent or paediatrician noticing tooth mobility or drifting in a 12–16-year-old, which is never normal and requires prompt dental evaluation.
Bleeding from gums that is disproportionate or localised to the molar regions in an otherwise hygiene-conscious teenager.
Family history of early tooth loss in a parent or sibling — a strong predictor of LAP or GAP in a child, warranting proactive periodontal probing and radiographs at the first adolescent dental visit.
A dental panoramic X-ray in a 12–14-year-old showing bone loss around the mesial aspect of the first permanent molar before significant plaque deposits are present.
emergencySigns: Acute lateral periodontal abscess with facial swelling, trismus (difficulty opening the mouth), and fever from suppurative infection within a deep periodontal pocket.
Rapid tooth mobility progressing to near-exfoliation of multiple permanent teeth in an adolescent with suspected or confirmed Papillon-Lefèvre syndrome or neutrophil disorder.

Systemic illness — persistent fevers, recurrent infections, lymphadenopathy — alongside severe periodontal destruction in a child, suggesting an undiagnosed immunodeficiency syndrome requiring urgent systemic evaluation.

causes.description: Highly leukotoxic JP2 strains of Aa specifically target and kill the patient's neutrophils and monocytes, crippling the primary innate immune defence against periodontal bacteria. The JP2 clone is found in approximately 25–30% of LAP patients of African descent in Western countries, while non-JP2 leukotoxic strains predominate in other ethnic groups. Aa colonises the supra- and subgingival surfaces and family members are often co-colonised.

Multiple studies have demonstrated reduced chemotactic migration of neutrophils toward bacterial products in LAP patients and their unaffected siblings, indicating a familial innate immune deficiency. Neutrophils reach the sulcus slowly or in inadequate numbers, allowing Aa and other pathogens to establish subgingival biofilms without adequate suppression. Several candidate gene variants in Fc-gamma receptors and chemokine receptors have been identified.

Beyond neutrophil defects, monocytes of LAP/GAP patients produce abnormally high levels of prostaglandin E2 and interleukin-1β in response to bacterial lipopolysaccharide, creating an exaggerated local inflammatory cytokine response that amplifies bone resorption beyond what the direct bacterial insult alone would cause.

causes.cause: Porphyromonas gingivalis and co-pathogens in GAP
causes.description: In generalised aggressive periodontitis, P. gingivalis — which evades complement and disrupts the cytokine signalling between neutrophils and the periodontium — is a key pathogen alongside Aa, T. forsythia, and Treponema denticola. Their combined virulence factors generate a dysbiotic subgingival microbiome that sustains rapid bone destruction.

LAP follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern with incomplete penetrance in many families; a sibling of an affected individual has approximately a 40–50% higher probability of developing the condition. Candidate loci affecting FcγRIIa receptor polymorphisms, interleukin-1 gene cluster variants, and catechol-O-methyltransferase activity have been identified in association studies, though no single causative gene explains all cases.

riskFactors.description: The familial aggregation of aggressive periodontitis is among the strongest risk predictors; having an affected parent or sibling raises personal risk by 3–5 fold, and siblings of LAP patients should receive proactive periodontal screening at puberty.

Carriage of the highly leukotoxic JP2 clone of Aa — transmitted between family members via saliva — is a strong independent predictor of LAP onset in adolescents; studies in Morocco found that JP2 colonisation preceded clinical disease by months to years.

Population studies consistently show higher prevalence of aggressive periodontitis in adolescents of African and Middle Eastern descent compared with European-origin populations, likely reflecting higher JP2 clone prevalence and possibly underlying genetic susceptibility polymorphisms.

Disease onset is closely tied to puberty, suggesting a hormonal modulation of the host-pathogen interaction; the specific first-molar/incisor distribution reflects the eruption sequence, as these are the first permanent teeth to provide a subgingival niche for Aa colonisation.

Poorly controlled diabetes impairs neutrophil function and cytokine signalling, amplifying periodontal destruction in young patients with aggressive periodontitis; there is strong bidirectional evidence that uncontrolled diabetes worsens periodontal outcomes, and that severe periodontitis worsens glycaemic control.

Tobacco smoking impairs neutrophil function, reduces gingival vascularity (masking clinical inflammation), and suppresses the antibody response to Aa; smoking is associated with more severe and treatment-resistant aggressive periodontitis and substantially worsens the prognosis of teeth already affected.

Papillon-Lefèvre syndrome (cathepsin C mutations), Chédiak-Higashi syndrome, cyclic neutropenia, and leukocyte adhesion deficiency all cause early-onset severe periodontitis that is clinically indistinguishable from aggressive periodontitis but associated with primary loss of the deciduous dentition in early childhood.

affectedDemographics: Adolescents aged 11–17, particularly those with family history of early tooth loss
Young adults aged 17–35 with generalised aggressive periodontitis
Populations of African and Middle Eastern descent with higher JP2 Aa clone prevalence
Children aged 2–5 with systemic immunodeficiency syndromes (Papillon-Lefèvre, severe neutropenia)
Adolescents and young adults with poorly controlled type 1 or type 2 diabetes
Families with known mutations in neutrophil function or Fc receptor polymorphisms
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis rests on a combination of clinical probing, radiographic assessment, and microbiological analysis, integrated with the patient's age, systemic health, and family history. The classic clinical picture is a 12–16-year-old with deep pockets and bone loss disproportionate to plaque levels, concentrating at first molars and incisors. Full-mouth periodontal charting (probing depth, attachment level, bleeding on probing at all six sites per tooth) provides the clinical foundation. Periapical and panoramic radiographs are essential: the pathognomonic finding in LAP is vertical, angular bone loss at the mesial surface of the first permanent molar, often mirror-image in both jaws, in an otherwise minimally affected dentition. In GAP, bone loss is generalised and less patterned. Cone-beam CT provides three-dimensional bone defect mapping for surgical planning. Microbiological testing — using real-time PCR-based subgingival plaque analysis — identifies Aa, P. gingivalis, T. forsythia, and T. denticola, which guides antibiotic selection; standard culture is being replaced by validated multiplex PCR kits in specialist centres. When a systemic immunodeficiency is suspected — particularly when the primary dentition was also severely affected, or when the child is under age 10 — urgent referral for haematological workup (full blood count with differential, neutrophil function testing) and immunological evaluation is required. Serum IgG antibody titres to Aa are elevated in 60–80% of LAP patients and can support the diagnosis or monitor antibody response to treatment. The principal differential diagnoses are chronic periodontitis (older age, plaque-proportionate bone loss, slower progression), necrotising periodontal disease (acute onset, necrotic interdental papillae, fever), and Papillon-Lefèvre syndrome (primary dentition also lost, skin hyperkeratosis).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures pocket depths and clinical attachment levels at all teeth to map the distribution and severity of bone loss; the hallmark finding in LAP is deep pocketing at first molars and incisors in an adolescent with otherwise healthy periodontium.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blunt probe is gently inserted at six points around each tooth to measure the depth of the gum pocket in millimetres; the procedure takes about 10–15 minutes and causes mild pressure but no significant pain.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects and quantifies alveolar bone loss; the characteristic angular bone loss at the mesial of first molars in a mirror-image distribution is the radiographic hallmark of localised aggressive periodontitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Periapical films use a small sensor inside the mouth; panoramic imaging uses a rotating machine around the head. Both are quick and involve very low radiation doses.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies specific periodontal pathogens — particularly Aa, P. gingivalis, T. forsythia — in the subgingival biofilm, which confirms the aggressive periodontitis microbial profile and guides antibiotic selection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A sterile paper point is placed into the pocket for 30–60 seconds to absorb subgingival fluid; the sample is sent to a dental microbiology laboratory and results return in 1–2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count with differential and neutrophil function test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for an underlying systemic immunodeficiency (neutropenia, Chédiak-Higashi, leukocyte adhesion deficiency) when disease onset is very early, affects the primary dentition, or is accompanied by systemic features.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venous blood draw; a haematologist may order additional neutrophil chemotaxis or phagocytosis assays requiring specialised laboratory analysis.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum IgG antibody to Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans
diagnosticTests.purpose: Elevated Aa-specific IgG is found in 60–80% of LAP patients and serves as a supportive diagnostic marker and an index of immune response to the primary pathogen; declining titres after treatment indicate pathogen reduction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw processed by an immunology or periodontology laboratory; results typically available within one week.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides three-dimensional mapping of infrabony defects and furcation involvement before surgical periodontal therapy, and assesses residual bone for future implant planning in cases where teeth will be lost.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Machine rotates around the head over 10–40 seconds with a chin rest; higher radiation than standard dental films but far lower than medical CT. Results are reviewed by the surgeon before treatment.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of aggressive periodontitis requires a more aggressive protocol than chronic periodontitis because the underlying immune deficit allows the key pathogen Aa to persist after mechanical debridement alone, leading to disease recurrence. The evidence-based protocol combines full-mouth subgingival debridement (scaling and root planing) — performed across all affected sites within a short window of 24–48 hours to prevent bacterial recolonisation from untreated sites — with systemic antibiotics prescribed simultaneously. The antibiotic of choice is the combination of amoxicillin (500 mg three times daily) and metronidazole (250–500 mg three times daily) for 7–14 days, which has robust evidence from multiple randomised controlled trials for suppression of Aa and significant improvement in attachment levels beyond debridement alone. For patients allergic to penicillin, azithromycin is an alternative. Systemic antibiotics are essential and not optional in aggressive periodontitis because mechanical debridement alone rarely eliminates Aa from the periodontal pockets and surrounding oral tissues. Where initial therapy achieves inadequate pocket reduction (residual pockets ≥6 mm), periodontal surgery — open flap debridement or regenerative procedures with bone grafts and guided tissue regeneration membranes — is performed 3–6 months after initial therapy to access deep defects. Bone regeneration with enamel matrix derivative (EMD) or demineralised bone matrix can partially restore lost attachment in selected infrabony defects. Early extraction of teeth with hopeless prognosis — due to severe bone loss, furcation involvement, or poor crown-to-root ratio — is recommended to eliminate reservoirs of Aa that would otherwise re-infect treated sites. Dental implants are highly successful in aggressive periodontitis patients who have maintained periodontal stability, though regular monitoring for peri-implantitis is needed. After active treatment, intensive supportive periodontal therapy (SPT) every 3 months is mandatory for life, as the underlying susceptibility does not resolve — irregular maintenance results in relapse in the majority.

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic amoxicillin plus metronidazole
medicalTreatments.description: The evidence-based first-line antibiotic combination for aggressive periodontitis, given simultaneously with full-mouth debridement. Amoxicillin (500 mg TDS) plus metronidazole (400–500 mg TDS) for 7–14 days targets Aa and co-pathogens residing in the periodontal pocket and adjacent soft tissues that mechanical instrumentation alone cannot reach.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Multiple RCTs show that adding this combination to scaling and root planing improves clinical attachment levels by an additional 1–2 mm and reduces pocket depth by 1–3 mm more than mechanical therapy alone, with approximately 70–85% of patients showing clinically meaningful improvement.

medicalTreatments.name: Full-mouth scaling and root planing
medicalTreatments.description: Mechanical debridement of subgingival plaque and calculus from all periodontal pockets, removing the bacterial biofilm and endotoxin-contaminated root surface. In aggressive periodontitis, the full mouth is treated within 24–48 hours to prevent re-colonisation of treated sites from untreated pockets.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Scaling and root planing alone reduces pocket depth by 0.5–2 mm and improves attachment by 0.5–1.5 mm at 3–6 months; combination with antibiotics adds a clinically significant increment to these outcomes in aggressive periodontitis.

medicalTreatments.name: Azithromycin (for penicillin-allergic patients)
medicalTreatments.description: A macrolide antibiotic with anti-inflammatory properties and tissue penetration into gingival crevicular fluid; used as an alternative to amoxicillin-metronidazole in penicillin-allergic aggressive periodontitis patients at 500 mg daily for 3–5 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Studies show improvement in clinical parameters comparable to amoxicillin-metronidazole in non-JP2 Aa strains; evidence is weaker for JP2-strain-associated LAP.

medicalTreatments.name: Supportive periodontal therapy (SPT) every 3 months
medicalTreatments.description: Intensive recall programme with subgingival debridement, assessment of pocket depths, radiographic monitoring, and oral hygiene reinforcement at 3-monthly intervals, adjusted to the individual risk level. Sustained lifelong maintenance is the most important determinant of long-term tooth retention in aggressive periodontitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Patients maintained on 3-monthly SPT retain significantly more teeth over 10 years than those with irregular maintenance; tooth loss rates in compliant patients fall from approximately 0.4 to 0.1 teeth per person per year.

surgicalOptions.name: Open flap debridement (periodontal surgery)
surgicalOptions.description: The gum is reflected back to give direct visual and instrument access to deep infrabony defects that cannot be adequately debrided by closed instrumentation; the root surface and bone defect are thoroughly cleaned before suturing the flap back in place. Performed 3–6 months after initial antibiotic-based therapy when pockets remain 6 mm or greater.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Additional attachment gain of 1–2 mm and pocket reduction of 1–3 mm beyond initial therapy; 80–90% of treated sites maintain clinical stability at 1 year in compliant patients.

surgicalOptions.name: Guided tissue regeneration (GTR) and bone grafting
surgicalOptions.description: A resorbable or non-resorbable barrier membrane and/or bone graft (autograft, allograft, or xenograft) is placed in the infrabony defect to exclude fast-growing gingival tissue and allow slower-growing bone and periodontal ligament cells to repopulate the defect. Indicated for three-wall infrabony defects with good residual bone support.
surgicalOptions.successRate: GTR achieves additional attachment gain of 2–4 mm over open flap alone in suitable defects; enamel matrix derivative (Emdogain) shows comparable regenerative outcomes in meta-analyses.

surgicalOptions.name: Dental implant placement (after stabilisation)
surgicalOptions.description: Implants are placed to replace teeth lost to aggressive periodontitis once the remaining periodontium has been stabilised and the patient has demonstrated compliance with maintenance therapy. Implant placement in a patient with active disease carries high failure risk.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year implant survival of approximately 90–95% in agressive periodontitis patients who are in active maintenance; slightly lower than in periodontally healthy patients, emphasising the need for rigorous follow-up.

alternativeTreatments.name: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) as adjunct
alternativeTreatments.description: Application of a photosensitising dye to the pocket followed by low-power laser light to generate reactive oxygen species that kill periodontal pathogens. Used as an adjunct to scaling in some centres; evidence shows modest additional pocket reduction but is insufficient to replace systemic antibiotics in aggressive periodontitis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Host modulation therapy (sub-antimicrobial dose doxycycline)
alternativeTreatments.description: Doxycycline at 20 mg twice daily — below the antimicrobial threshold — inhibits matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) that drive collagen and bone degradation in the periodontal tissues. A useful adjunct in patients with generalised disease or systemic risk factors, though it does not treat the infection and is an adjunct to, not replacement for, mechanical therapy and antibiotics.

whySeeSpecialist: Aggressive periodontitis in an adolescent must be managed by a periodontist or specialist in restorative dentistry with periodontal expertise. The condition is under-recognised in general dental practice, and delay of even 6–12 months can mean the difference between saving or losing the first permanent molars. A periodontist experienced in the full-mouth debridement-plus-antibiotics protocol for Aa-associated disease, with access to microbiological testing and periodontal surgery, offers the best outcomes. Siblings of affected patients should receive periodontal screening as part of the same specialist visit.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a periodontist trained in aggressive periodontitis management, with access to subgingival microbiological PCR testing and guided tissue regeneration surgery. Ask whether the practice uses a full-mouth debridement protocol with simultaneous systemic antibiotics — the evidence-based standard — rather than quadrant-by-quadrant debridement alone. Ensure the practice offers 3-monthly supportive maintenance. For adolescents, a periodontist comfortable collaborating with a paediatric dentist or orthodontist provides the most coordinated care.
preventionStrategies: Screen all siblings and children of affected patients with full periodontal probing and panoramic radiography at the first permanent molar eruption (around age 7) to allow treatment before severe bone loss occurs.

Achieve excellent plaque control with a powered toothbrush, interdental brushing, and consistent attendance; while aggressive periodontitis occurs despite reasonable hygiene, good plaque control reduces disease severity and rate of progression.
Avoid tobacco smoking entirely — smoking dramatically worsens both the progression and treatment response in aggressive periodontitis.
Maintain tight glycaemic control in diabetic patients, as uncontrolled diabetes doubles the rate of periodontal bone loss.
Attend 3-monthly supportive periodontal therapy throughout life after active treatment; this is the most proven strategy for preventing relapse and tooth loss.
lifestyleModifications: Use a powered toothbrush for 2 minutes twice daily and a single-tufted brush to clean the gingival margin around first molars and incisors, the sites most vulnerable to disease recurrence.
Use interdental brushes or dental floss daily between every tooth; microbial re-colonisation of pockets begins within days of debridement when home hygiene is neglected.

Quit smoking immediately — the clinical response to periodontal treatment in smokers is approximately 50% worse than in non-smokers, and smoking masks gingival inflammation, delaying recognition of relapse.
Eat a low-sugar, anti-inflammatory diet rich in omega-3 fatty acids, which may modulate the exaggerated monocytic prostaglandin response that amplifies bone destruction in aggressive periodontitis.
Do not skip recall appointments — compliance with 3-monthly professional debridement is the single most important lifestyle decision for retaining teeth over the long term.

recommended: Omega-3 fatty acid-rich foods (oily fish, walnuts, flaxseed) for their anti-inflammatory properties relevant to the exaggerated cytokine response in aggressive periodontitis
High-fibre vegetables and fruits that require chewing and stimulate gingival blood flow
Vitamin C-rich foods to support collagen synthesis in the periodontal ligament
Adequate vitamin D and calcium for alveolar bone maintenance, particularly important given the bone loss the condition causes
avoid: Tobacco in all forms — the most important dietary and lifestyle modification for periodontal outcomes
High-sugar foods and beverages that promote dental caries and shift the subgingival microbiome toward pathogenic species
Excessive alcohol, which impairs immune function and neutrophil activity already compromised in aggressive periodontitis

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise is beneficial for all patients with aggressive periodontitis, as physical fitness supports immune function, improves insulin sensitivity in diabetic patients, and reduces systemic inflammation. There are no exercise restrictions related to periodontosis itself. After periodontal surgery, avoid vigorous exercise for 48–72 hours to minimise bleeding and swelling; your periodontist will advise on specific restrictions based on the procedure performed.
dailyManagement: Perform thorough interdental cleaning and careful brushing of the gingival margins twice daily — the standard of care for maintaining the gains achieved by active treatment.
Attend every 3-month supportive periodontal therapy appointment without exception; skipping even one maintenance visit has been shown to allow measurable pocket re-deepening.
Monitor for tooth mobility, drifting, or increased gap between the front teeth, which may signal disease reactivation between appointments.
Control any systemic conditions that amplify disease, particularly diabetes; keep your periodontist and physician in communication about your glycaemic control.
Inform all family members who share your household — siblings and children — that they should be screened by a periodontist, as Aa is transmitted within families.
prognosis: With early diagnosis and the full-mouth debridement plus systemic antibiotic protocol followed by lifelong 3-monthly maintenance, the long-term prognosis for aggressive periodontitis is significantly better than if treated as conventional chronic periodontitis. Studies of specialist-managed aggressive periodontitis patients in active maintenance show tooth loss rates of 0.06–0.15 teeth per person per year — comparable to well-treated chronic periodontitis — versus 0.4–1.0 teeth per person per year without specialist care. However, the prognosis is highly patient-dependent: smokers, patients with poor compliance, and those with systemic immunodeficiency fare considerably worse. Teeth with greater than 50–60% bone loss at presentation have a guarded long-term prognosis even with optimal treatment, and extraction of hopeless teeth in the context of overall rehabilitation planning often achieves better functional outcomes than prolonged retention of teeth that serve as bacterial reservoirs. Dental implants placed after successful periodontal stabilisation achieve outcomes close to those in periodontally healthy patients, providing a reliable long-term solution for lost teeth.
recoveryTimeline: Symptoms of acute periodontal abscess resolve within 5–10 days of drainage and antibiotic therapy. Measurable improvement in pocket depths is seen at the 3-month post-treatment review. Maximum clinical attachment gain from initial therapy is assessed at 3–6 months, when the decision for periodontal surgery is made. Surgical sites heal over 4–6 weeks, with radiographic evidence of bone fill in regeneration procedures appearing at 6–12 months. Disease control is a lifelong maintenance commitment rather than a time-limited treatment, as the underlying genetic susceptibility persists indefinitely.
complications: Early tooth loss — the primary consequence of unrecognised or inadequately treated aggressive periodontitis; the first permanent molars, which are the most important teeth for chewing, are most at risk and are irreplaceable if lost in adolescence without implant rehabilitation.

Periodontal abscess — acute suppurative infection within a deep pocket causing severe pain, swelling, and fever; requires emergency drainage and may necessitate tooth extraction if the tooth is not salvageable.

Pathological tooth migration (drifting) — as anterior teeth lose supporting bone, they drift forward under lip pressure, creating unsightly gaps and bite problems that may require combined periodontal and orthodontic treatment to correct.

Peri-implantitis — after implants are placed, the same genetic susceptibility that caused the original periodontitis can predispose to infection around implants; more frequent maintenance is required than for periodontally healthy patients.

Psychological impact — adolescents with visible tooth mobility, drifting, or missing front teeth experience significant negative effects on self-esteem and social confidence, making early treatment both a medical and psychosocial priority.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Periodontology — Aggressive Periodontitis
supportResources.description: Patient information on aggressive periodontitis causes, diagnosis, and treatment from the leading U.S. periodontology professional society.

supportResources.name: EFP — European Federation of Periodontology Patient Guide
supportResources.description: Plain-language patient information on periodontal diseases and treatment from the European Federation of Periodontology.

supportResources.name: CDC — Periodontal Disease
supportResources.description: U.S. CDC epidemiological overview of periodontal disease, including risk factors and systemic health connections.

sources.title: Tonetti MS, Greenwell H, Kornman KS. Staging and grading of periodontitis: framework and proposal of a new classification. J Periodontol. 2018
Albandar JM et al. Global epidemiology of aggressive periodontitis. Periodontol 2000. 2014
Herrera D et al. Adjunctive effect of systemic amoxicillin and metronidazole to mechanical debridement in aggressive periodontitis. J Clin Periodontol. 2008
Schenkein HA. Finding genetic risk factors for periodontal diseases. Periodontol 2000. 2002
Baelum V, Lopez R. Periodontal disease epidemiology — learned and unlearned? Periodontol 2000. 2013

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic periodontitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Chronic periodontitis is plaque-proportionate, progresses slowly over decades, and is rare before age 30; aggressive periodontitis presents in adolescents with bone loss disproportionate to plaque levels and a characteristic molar/incisor distribution.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute gingivitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Gingivitis is confined to the gingival soft tissue without bone loss; aggressive periodontitis involves true bone destruction detectable on radiographs. Gingivitis is fully reversible; bone loss in aggressive periodontitis is permanent without regenerative surgery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Dental abscess
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A dental abscess from pulp necrosis produces periapical bone loss at the root tip; periodontal pocketing in aggressive periodontitis produces lateral bone destruction around the root. Pulp testing distinguishes a vital tooth with periodontitis from a non-vital tooth with an apical abscess.

relatedConditions.name: Localised aggressive periodontitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The localised form of the same disease entity — periodontosis/aggressive periodontitis — restricted to first molars and incisors, representing the classic juvenile periodontitis pattern.

relatedConditions.name: Generalised aggressive periodontitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The generalised form of the same disease affecting most or all teeth in young adults, sharing the same microbial and genetic drivers as the localised form but with wider distribution.

relatedConditions.name: Generalised chronic periodontitis
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common form of periodontal disease, distinguished from aggressive periodontitis by its older age of onset, slower progression, and proportionate relationship with plaque burden — important in the differential diagnosis.

relatedConditions.name: Type 2 diabetes with periodontal disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Diabetes is a major systemic amplifier of aggressive periodontitis severity; bidirectional evidence shows that uncontrolled blood sugar worsens bone loss and severe periodontitis impairs glycaemic control.

faqs.question: What is periodontosis?
faqs.answer: Periodontosis — also called aggressive periodontitis or juvenile periodontitis — is a rapidly destructive form of gum disease that starts in adolescence, attacking the bone supporting the teeth far faster than ordinary gum disease. It concentrates around the first permanent molars and front teeth and can cause tooth loss before age 25 without early treatment.

faqs.question: How is periodontosis different from ordinary gum disease?
faqs.answer: Ordinary (chronic) gum disease is driven by plaque build-up and progresses slowly over decades, mainly in adults over 40. Periodontosis starts at puberty, destroys bone rapidly despite comparatively little plaque, runs in families, and is driven by a specific bacterium (Aa) and an inherited neutrophil function defect rather than poor hygiene alone.

faqs.question: What causes periodontosis?
faqs.answer: Periodontosis is caused by a combination of a specific oral bacterium called Aggregatibacter actinomycetemcomitans (Aa) — which kills the white blood cells normally defending the gums — and a genetic defect in those white blood cells that means they cannot respond adequately. Risk is inherited, with about 40–50% of patients having an affected first-degree relative.

faqs.question: At what age does periodontosis start?
faqs.answer: Periodontosis typically begins at puberty, around ages 11–17, when the first permanent molars and incisors provide the first long-term subgingival surfaces for the causative bacteria to colonise. The localised form usually appears between ages 12 and 16; the generalised form tends to present in young adults aged 17–35.

faqs.question: How is periodontosis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment combines full-mouth scaling and root planing — cleaning all pockets within 24–48 hours — with simultaneous systemic antibiotics, usually amoxicillin plus metronidazole for 7–14 days. This combination eliminates the specific bacterium that mechanical cleaning alone cannot reach. Periodontal surgery and bone grafting may be needed for deep defects.

faqs.question: Is periodontosis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. Periodontosis runs strongly in families with an autosomal dominant pattern in many affected families. Having a parent or sibling with the condition raises your own risk 3–5 fold. Siblings and children of affected patients should be screened with periodontal probing and radiographs at the time the first permanent molars erupt.

faqs.question: Can periodontosis be cured?
faqs.answer: Periodontosis cannot be permanently cured because the underlying genetic susceptibility remains. However, with specialist treatment — combined antibiotic and mechanical therapy — followed by lifelong 3-monthly maintenance visits, most patients retain their teeth long-term with tooth loss rates comparable to well-treated chronic periodontitis.

faqs.question: What does periodontosis look like on a dental X-ray?
faqs.answer: The classic X-ray finding is vertical (angular) bone loss at the mesial surface of the first permanent molar — the side closest to the front teeth — often in a mirror-image pattern in both jaws. This bone loss is out of proportion to the amount of plaque visible and is highly characteristic when seen in a teenager.

faqs.question: Does periodontosis cause tooth loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated or inadequately treated aggressive periodontitis can destroy most of the bone supporting the first molars and front teeth within 2–5 years, leading to tooth mobility and eventual loss before the mid-twenties. With specialist care and consistent maintenance, the rate of tooth loss is dramatically reduced.

faqs.question: How is periodontosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A periodontist diagnoses periodontosis through periodontal probing (measuring pocket depths), periapical and panoramic radiographs showing the characteristic angular bone loss pattern, and microbiological testing identifying Aa and other specific bacteria. The patient's age and family history complete the diagnostic picture.

faqs.question: Can diabetics develop periodontosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Diabetes impairs the same neutrophil function that is already deficient in aggressive periodontitis, dramatically worsening bone destruction. The relationship is bidirectional: uncontrolled diabetes accelerates periodontal bone loss, and severe periodontitis impairs glycaemic control. Tight blood sugar management is essential for anyone with aggressive periodontitis and diabetes.

faqs.question: Why does periodontosis start at the first molars?
faqs.answer: The first permanent molars erupt at around age 6 — the earliest permanent teeth to create a subgingival crevice — and are the first sites where Aa can establish a long-term subgingival colony. By puberty, colonisation is well established around the molars and incisors, producing the characteristic distribution of bone loss.

faqs.question: Does smoking worsen periodontosis?
faqs.answer: Significantly. Smoking impairs neutrophil function — already compromised in aggressive periodontitis — reduces gingival vascularity (masking visible inflammation so disease goes unrecognised longer), and suppresses the immune response to the causative bacteria. Smokers with aggressive periodontitis respond about 50% less well to treatment and have far higher tooth loss rates.

faqs.question: Are antibiotics necessary for treating periodontosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Unlike chronic periodontitis where mechanical cleaning can be sufficient, aggressive periodontitis requires systemic antibiotics — typically amoxicillin plus metronidazole — because the key pathogen Aa invades gingival soft tissues and cannot be eliminated by scaling alone. Omitting antibiotics results in significantly worse outcomes and higher relapse rates.

faqs.question: How often do I need dental appointments after periodontosis treatment?
faqs.answer: Every 3 months, indefinitely. The genetic susceptibility that caused aggressive periodontitis does not resolve after treatment, so pockets can re-deepen if professional debridement is not maintained on a 3-monthly schedule. Studies consistently show that patients who skip maintenance appointments lose significantly more teeth over 10 years than those who attend regularly.

faqs.question: Can dental implants be placed after periodontosis?
faqs.answer: Yes, but only after the remaining periodontium has been stabilised and the patient has demonstrated consistent compliance with maintenance therapy. Implant survival in well-managed periodontitis patients is approximately 90–95% at 5 years, slightly lower than in periodontally healthy people, requiring more vigilant professional monitoring for peri-implantitis.

faqs.question: Should my children be screened if I have periodontosis?
faqs.answer: Yes, promptly. Aa is transmitted between family members in saliva, and the genetic susceptibility is also heritable. Children of affected parents should receive periodic probing and panoramic X-ray assessment from the time the first permanent molars erupt at age 6–7 so that any early bone loss can be treated before it becomes severe.

faqs.question: What is the difference between localised and generalised aggressive periodontitis?
faqs.answer: Localised aggressive periodontitis is confined to the first permanent molars and incisors in an adolescent. Generalised aggressive periodontitis affects most or all teeth and typically presents in young adults aged 17–35, with episodic active phases and multiple pathogens involved alongside Aa. Both forms require the same antibiotic-combined treatment protocol.

faqs.question: Can periodontosis affect primary (baby) teeth?
faqs.answer: Classic aggressive periodontitis affects permanent teeth. However, children with systemic immunodeficiency syndromes — such as Papillon-Lefèvre syndrome, neutropenia, or leukocyte adhesion deficiency — can lose their primary teeth to a similar destructive process from age 2–5. Premature primary tooth loss should prompt urgent haematological and immunological investigation.

metaDescription: Periodontosis is rapid bone-destroying gum disease in adolescents, caused by bacterial and genetic factors. Learn symptoms, antibiotic treatment and prevention.
